growth_records.cite: Madin JS et al. A synthesis of bacterial and archaeal phenotypic trait data. Sci Data 7:170 (2020); source dataset: engqvist.
growth_records.notes: Species-level trait record; culture mode/pH often unspecified; medium + uptake/secretion pending literature curation.

growth_records.cite: Madin JS et al. A synthesis of bacterial and archaeal phenotypic trait data. Sci Data 7:170 (2020); source dataset: pasteur.
growth_records.notes: Species-level trait record; culture mode/pH often unspecified; medium + uptake/secretion pending literature curation.

up.units: relative flux units (normalized; succinate uptake fixed at 100 in MFA)

mfa.note: All net fluxes estimated relative to succinate uptake fixed at 100 units; this condition (Fig 7A) is the reference to which Fig 7B/7C fold-changes are compared. Individual central-carbon split fluxes (succinate->fumarate incl. decarboxylating TCA arm, citrate synthase, malic enzyme, etc.) are shown in Fig 7A and tabulated in data S9 (net flux data), NOT in the XML text.

growth_records.cite: Carolin C. M. Schulte et al. Metabolic control of nitrogen fixation in rhizobium-legume symbioses. Science Advances (2021). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.abh2433. [PMC8324050]
growth_records.snippet: Metabolic flux analysis of A. caulinodans ORS571 ... grown in UMS medium containing 20% [13C4]-succinate and 80% unlabeled sodium succinate. ... The fluxes were estimated relative to the succinate uptake flux fixed at 100 units.
growth_records.notes: 13C-MFA reference condition (Fig 7A): free-living non-diazotrophic, 3% O2, NH4Cl, 37 degC. Only the normalization value (succinate uptake = 100 relative units) is an explicit number in text; all other net fluxes are figure-locked (Fig 7A) / in supplementary data S9 which is not available here. No absolute mmol/gDW/h rate reported. PHB synthesis ~3-fold higher under 3% O2 vs aerobic (stated qualitatively). growth_rate not reported.

mfa.note: Fig 7B: TCA cycle fluxes increased during free-living diazotrophic growth vs non-diazotrophic (Fig 7A), consistent with higher reductant demand for N2 fixation and growth. Fold-changes color-coded in Fig 7B; absolute/relative flux numbers figure-locked and in data S9.

growth_records.cite: Carolin C. M. Schulte et al. Metabolic control of nitrogen fixation in rhizobium-legume symbioses. Science Advances (2021). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.abh2433. [PMC8324050]
growth_records.snippet: For diazotrophic growth, cultures were continuously sparged with a gas mixture containing 97% N2 and 3% O2 ... TCA cycle fluxes increased during free-living diazotrophic growth compared to non-diazotrophic growth
growth_records.notes: 13C-MFA of free-living diazotrophic A. caulinodans (Fig 7B), 97% N2 / 3% O2, 37 degC, N2 as sole N source. Only normalization succinate=100 explicit; central-carbon fluxes figure-locked/data S9. TCA flux up vs non-diazotrophic (qualitative).

growth_records.medium: in planta - root nodules of Sesbania rostrata

mfa.note: Fig 7C bacteroids: overall down-regulation of TCA cycle vs non-diazotrophic microaerobic growth; strongest down-regulation of the decarboxylating arm (citrate->succinate), limiting 2-oxoglutarate/ammonia assimilation. Fold-change flux values figure-locked (Fig 7C) and in data S9.

growth_records.cite: Carolin C. M. Schulte et al. Metabolic control of nitrogen fixation in rhizobium-legume symbioses. Science Advances (2021). DOI:10.1126/sciadv.abh2433. [PMC8324050]
growth_records.snippet: bacteroids isolated from root nodules of S. rostrata ... an overall down-regulation of the TCA cycle was observed when comparing bacteroid metabolism to non-diazotrophic growth under microaerobic conditions. The highest relative down-regula
growth_records.notes: 13C-MFA of symbiotic A. caulinodans bacteroids from S. rostrata nodules (Fig 7C), 21 plants pooled as 3 replicates. In planta symbiotic state (culture_mode=co-culture as closest allowed term; not a defined broth). Only succinate=100 normalization explicit; all split fluxes figure-locked/data S9. Down-regulated TCA, esp. decarboxylating arm (qualitative).
